exploration_log.aha_moment: FileAttributes uses ltrim (strip leading slash only) while DirectoryAttributes uses trim (strip both ends). The fix mirrors exactly what each constructor does.
exploration_log.hindsight: Nothing to change; straightforward fix in both classes.

key_decisions.decision: Apply the same normalization inline (ltrim or trim) to match constructor behavior
key_decisions.reasoning: Calling the constructor would reset all fields; inline normalization is the minimal correct change

search_sequence.result: FileAttributes constructor does ltrim($this->path, '/'); DirectoryAttributes constructor does trim($this->path, '/'); withPath in both assigns raw path

solve_notes: Applied the same path normalization in `withPath` that each constructor already performs: `ltrim($path, '/')` in `FileAttributes` and `trim($path, '/')` in `DirectoryAttributes`. This ensures `path()` never returns a path starting (or ending, for directories) with a slash regardless of how the instance was created.
task_text:
Fix FileAttributes and DirectoryAttributes.withPath bypassing constructor path normalization

In `src/FileAttributes.php` and `src/DirectoryAttributes.php`, the
`withPath(string $path)` method assigns the provided string directly
to `$clone->path` without applying the same normalization performed
by the constructor. `FileAttributes::__construct` does
`ltrim($this->path, '/')` and `DirectoryAttributes::__construct`
does `trim($this->path, '/')`. A call such as
`$attrs->withPath('/dir/file.txt')` therefore produces an instance
where `path()` returns `'/dir/file.txt'` instead of `'dir/file.txt'`,
breaking the invariant that stored paths never begin (or end, for
directories) with a slash. Fix `withPath` in both classes to apply
the same path-stripping normalization as their respective
constructors.

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set already covers everything needed: the two edited methods and their constructors which define the normalization to apply. An AI agent could implement the fix correctly from just these four defs without needing any additional context.
